retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate is split into two separate sugar molecules, dihydroxyacetone phosphate and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, by aldolase .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose-6-phosphate is isomerized by phosphoglucose isomerase to form fructose-6-phosphate.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The steps of glycolysis are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The molecule of dihydroxyacetone phosphate is isomerized by triosephosphate isomerase to form a second glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate is phosphorylated by glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase to form 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate. This step requires NAD+ as a cofactor.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Gluconeogenesis -- Cellular Level -- Gluconeogenesis Reactions And Enzymes
retrieved_docs.content: Aldolase combines glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate and DHAP to form fructose 1,6-bisphosphate.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate is converted to 3-phosphoglycerate by phosphoglycerate kinase . This step involves the transfer of a phosphate molecule to ADP to form 1 molecule of ATP.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Fructose-6-phosphate is phosphorylated by phosphofructokinase to form fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. This step requires one molecule of ATP.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: 2-phosphoglycerate is dehydrated to produce phosphoenolpyruvate by the enzyme enolase .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Aerobic Glycolysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycolysis is a central metabolic pathway that is used by all cells for the oxidation of glucose to generate energy in the form of ATP (Adenosine triphosphate) and intermediates for use in other metabolic pathways. Besides glucose, other hexose sugars such as fructose and galactose also end up in the glycolytic pathway for catabolism [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: 3-phosphoglycerate rearranges to form 2-phosphoglycerate by the enzyme phosphoglycerate mutase .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose gets phosphorylated by hexokinase , forming glucose-6-phosphate. This step requires one molecule of ATP.

retrieved_docs.content: Enolase hydrates PEP to form 2-phosphoglycerate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1341
retrieved_docs.content: Beta‑D‑glucopyranose is classified as a saccharide, belonging to the broad group of carbohydrate molecules known as saccharidy. As a specific sugar monomer, beta‑D‑glucopyranose exemplifies the structural and functional characteristics of saccharides, which serve as essential energy sources, metabolic intermediates, and structural components in biological systems. Recognizing beta‑D‑glucopyranose as a member of the saccharidy class aids in understanding its role in nutrition, biochemical pathways, and laboratory analyses of carbohydrate content.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 774
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyethylový škrob and beta‑D‑glucopyranose are both classified as saccharides, meaning they belong to the broader group of carbohydrate molecules. Hydroxyethylový škrob, a chemically modified starch, is used in medical settings as a plasma volume expander and shares the fundamental carbohydrate structure of saccharides. Beta‑D‑glucopyranose represents the cyclic form of glucose, a basic monosaccharide that serves as a primary energy source in human metabolism. Recognizing that both compounds are saccharides highlights their common biochemical nature while underscoring their distinct roles in clinical practice and nutritional physiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1621
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose 100 mg/mL injection is formulated with dextrose as its pharmacologically active component; anhydrous dextrose constitutes the active moiety of the preparation, while dextrose monohydrate serves as the active ingredient. Both forms of dextrose provide the therapeutic glucose delivered by the injection, ensuring consistent dosing and efficacy in clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 569
retrieved_docs.content: Anhydrous dextrose serves as the active moiety of the medication glucose 100 MG/ML Injection, meaning it is the pharmacologically effective component of this injectable glucose solution used to provide rapid carbohydrate supplementation in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 271
retrieved_docs.content: Anhydrous dextrose is the active moiety of the pharmaceutical preparation glucose 100 mg/mL Injection, providing the therapeutic effect of that injection. In the formulation identified as voda, glucose 100 mg/mL Injection is included as an inactive ingredient, contributing to the overall composition without exerting a direct pharmacologic action. This demonstrates how the same substance can serve as the active component in one product while being incorporated as an inactive component in another formulation.

Collectively, this integrated network underscores that immunosuppressants are essential tools for controlling interleukin‑mediated immune activation, are often derived from or characterized by specialized biosynthetic mechanisms, and interact with multiple metabolic and biosynthetic pathways—knowledge that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies and understanding the biochemical basis of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1663
retrieved_docs.content: Akarbóza is identified as a type of saccharide and is employed as a hypoglycemic agent to lower blood glucose levels. Migalastat is also classified within the saccharide category, indicating a chemical relationship to this group of carbohydrates. Together, these connections illustrate that certain saccharides, such as akarbóza, have direct therapeutic applications in managing hypoglycemia, while other saccharide‑related compounds like migalastat may possess distinct pharmacological roles. Recognizing the saccharide nature of these agents can inform their mechanisms of action and guide appropriate clinical use.
